item.title: 'Eua 8.6°C
item.description: A chilly 8.6° Celsius recorded in ‘Eua early this morning has broken the record for the lowest minimum temperature ever recorded in Tonga. The Tonga Meteorological Service observed the new record at 6:30 am today, 6 July 2026, recording a minimum temperature of 8.6°C at the Hango Automatic Weather Station in ‘Eua.
item.content:encoded:
A chilly 8.6° Celsius recorded in ‘Eua early this morning has broken the record for the lowest minimum temperature ever recorded in Tonga.

The Tonga Meteorological Service observed the new record at 6:30 am today, 6 July 2026, recording a minimum temperature of 8.6°C at the Hango Automatic Weather Station in ‘Eua.

This surpasses the previous record of 8.7°C, which was recorded at Fuaʻamotu on 8 September 1994.

" It is important to note that the previous lowest minimum temperature recorded in 1994 was an El Nino Year. The new national lowest minimum temperature being set here in 2026 is also an El Nino Year. This highlights a minimum temperature climatological correlation pattern and El Nino Years for Tonga," the Tonga Meteorological Services stated.

" The unusually cold conditions experienced across Tonga are associated with the current El Niño event that is present across the Pacific Ocean."

During El Niño, the presence and domination of the southeast trade winds circulate cooler air masses from the south and from mid-latitude regions in Tonga during this time of the year. This can result in colder nights and lower overnight temperatures, particularly during the dry season. Clear skies and light winds overnight also allow heat to escape more efficiently from the Earth's surface, leading to significantly cooler early morning temperatures.

Tonga Met advised the public, particularly the elderly, young children, and those with underlying health conditions, to take appropriate precautions during these cold conditions. Farmers are also encouraged to monitor livestock and crops that may be sensitive to unusually low temperatures.

item.title: One Tonga Choral Event warms Tonga with songs of praise
item.description: Entering its 16 th year, the ‘One Tonga, Glorifying God in Song, Choral Event’, presented 12 choirs and musical groups on stage at the Queen Salote Memorial Hall last night, 5 July. The annual event presented by Robina Nakao, the CEO of Fexco/ Western Union Tonga, brings together hundreds of singers from different Christian demominations to celebrate faith, unity and the annual official birthday o
item.content:encoded:
Entering its 16 th year, the ‘One Tonga, Glorifying God in Song, Choral Event’, presented 12 choirs and musical groups on stage at the Queen Salote Memorial Hall last night, 5 July.

The annual event presented by Robina Nakao, the CEO of Fexco/ Western Union Tonga, brings together hundreds of singers from different Christian demominations to celebrate faith, unity and the annual official birthday of King Tupou VI .

This year, several of the choirs and singing groups were smaller in numbers than in previous years, but just as full of passion and fervor in presenting their songs of praise.

Participating choirs included the Asssemblies of God, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, Siasi ' o Tonga, Seventh day Adventist Church, The Salvation Army, Free Church of Tonga, Sisu ko e Fetu'u Ngingila, Catholic Diocese of Tonga and Niue, Fijian Community, Siasi Tokaikolo 'ia Kalasi.

Tupou College Music Academy presented a new arrangement of the Hymn Halelu'ia Kuo Monu'ia Eni. The all-girls brass band from Queen Salote College, presented the final musical item.

Heads of the Christian churches attended.

The guest of honour, HM Queen Nanasipau'u, currently in New Zealand, watched the concert through a remote link.

The event attracted a full house, with the audience wrapped up well on one of Tonga's coldest July nights.

item.title: Cheering crowds greet Miss Heilala float parade
item.description: Newly crowned Miss Heilala 2026-27 Silia Sitoafa Matelau shone at the Miss Heilala annual float parade celebration in Nuku'alofa today, marking the final official event of this year's pageant. Miss Heilala and 13 other contestants were greeted by cheering crowds, families, friends and supporters who lined the streets, applauding and waving as the contestants travelled on decorated floats, beginnin
item.content:encoded:
Newly crowned Miss Heilala 2026-27 Silia Sitoafa Matelau shone at the Miss Heilala annual float parade celebration in Nuku'alofa today, marking the final official event of this year's pageant.

Miss Heilala and 13 other contestants were greeted by cheering crowds, families, friends and supporters who lined the streets, applauding and waving as the contestants travelled on decorated floats, beginning at St. Andrew's High School and along Taufa'ahau Road to Taufa'ahau Wharf.

Along with school marching bands, several government ministries, community organisations and businesses supported decorated floats, adding to the festive atmosphere of the event.

item.title: Silia Matelau (Miss Lady Maria Australia 2026) wins Miss Heilala 2026
item.description: Silia Matelau (22), was crowned Miss Heilala 2026 tonight, 3 July 2026, at the Miss Heilala Ball in the ‘Atele Indoor Stadium. The US -born Tongan, who entered the pageant as Miss Lady Maria Australia 2026, won all but one of the main judging categories. Silia received her sash from HRH the Crown Princess, Sinaitakala Tuku’aho, and was crowned by the former Miss Heilala, Siosi’ana Taumoepeau. The
item.content:encoded:
Silia Matelau (22), was crowned Miss Heilala 2026 tonight, 3 July 2026, at the Miss Heilala Ball in the ‘Atele Indoor Stadium. The US -born Tongan, who entered the pageant as Miss Lady Maria Australia 2026, won all but one of the main judging categories.

Silia received her sash from HRH the Crown Princess, Sinaitakala Tuku’aho, and was crowned by the former Miss Heilala, Siosi’ana Taumoepeau. The crowd was pleased with the result, with happy vibes throughout the announcement of the results, cheering loudly for Silia whenever she was called to receive an award. She won Miss Interview, Best Tau'olunga, Best Talent, and Best Sarong Wear, and was first runner-up in the island creation. She also won a special award for Best Teunga Tau'olunga Kakala (dancing costume).

With the Heilala crown, she also received $5000 pa'anga in prize money and two tickets from Tongatapu to any direct Fiji Airways destination.

MI ss Heilala will represent the Kingdom as Miss Tonga in the 2027 Miss Pacific Islands regional pageant.

Silia hails from the villages of Fo’ui, Fahefa, Kolomotu’a, Kolovai, Neiafu, and Niuafo’ou. Before travelling to Tonga to compete in the Heilala pageant, Silia worked at a restaurant in Florida, USA .

She has been ballet dancing since she was 10 years old and has some experience in modelling. She also has a passion for art, painting, and drawing.

The 14 contestants showed a close and loving bond as they celebrated each other's wins, and with the new Miss Heilala on stage, the girls ran toward each other and danced as the crowd joined with more happy cheers.

Assisting with the awards on stage were the Tourism Minister and Director of the Heilala Festival, Hon. Semisi Sika, Tourism CEO Sandradee Fifita, and other guests.

item.title: Kolomotu'a Eagles claim Pataloni Shield
item.description: The Kolomotu'a 'Ikale (Eagles) were awarded the Pataloni (Patrons) Shield as champions of the Tongatapu Sub-Union Pataloni title 2026 on Saturday after a closely contested grand final at Teufaiva Stadium. Kolomotu'a 'Ikale edged out Ha'ateiho Spartans 28-25 in the annual rugby union competition, which brings together clubs from across Tongatapu.
item.content:encoded:
The Kolomotu'a 'Ikale (Eagles) were awarded the Pataloni (Patrons) Shield as champions of the Tongatapu Sub-Union Pataloni title 2026 on Saturday after a closely contested grand final at Teufaiva Stadium.

Kolomotu'a 'Ikale edged out Ha'ateiho Spartans 28-25 in the annual rugby union competition, which brings together clubs from across Tongatapu.

Kolomotu'a prevailed in a head-to-head contest that remained in the balance until the closing stages, with the Eagles holding on to claim a three-point victory.

Supporters from both clubs packed Teufaiva Stadium, with strong backing for each side creating a vibrant atmosphere for the final.

The Pataloni Tournament is one of the Tongatapu Sub-Union's key competitions, providing local clubs with the opportunity to compete for the provincial title and showcase emerging rugby talent.

Following the final whistle, families and supporters of the Kolomotu'a side gathered on the field to celebrate the victory under clear and sunny conditions for the conclusion of another competitive season of sub-union rugby in Tongatapu.

item.title: Agricultural planning vital for food security during El Niño
item.description: Local authorities are encouraging farmers to plan and prepare for likely dry months ahead during El Niño season in Tonga, as a drought warning is in effect for the Ha'apai island groups, while the rest of Tonga is on a drought alert. Acting Director for the Ministry of Agriculture ( MAFF ) Dan Savia ‘Atuekaho stressed the risks of this El Niño to food security and livelihood for Tongans.
item.content:encoded:
Local authorities are encouraging farmers to plan and prepare for likely dry months ahead during El Niño season in Tonga, as a drought warning is in effect for the Ha'apai island groups, while the rest of Tonga is on a drought alert.

Acting Director for the Ministry of Agriculture ( MAFF ) Dan Savia ‘Atuekaho stressed the risks of this El Niño to food security and livelihood for Tongans.

‘Atuekaho told Matangi Tonga that farmers, fishermen, and households must be aware of the disaster that may follow.

" One of the biggest concern for Tonga is below-average rainfall. Most island groups, including Tongatapu, Vava’u and Ha’apai are expected to experience dry conditions," he said.

“The type of soil in Ha’apai is called sandy loam or known as tou’one. It is not able to retain water for a long period of time. Therefore, Ha’apai's agriculture is vulnerable [during drought] and differs from Tongatapu, which has a clay soil type. For Vava’u and other outer islands, the potential for water-holding capacity varies.”

He said there is also the risk of lower groundwater levels resulting in more saline soil, and if used for irrigation, it can damage crops.

He noted that key crops such a yam, cassava, taro, and watermelon are at risk of poor growth due to dry soils. He added that drought can lead to delayed planting season, lower crop yields, and increased pest and disease stress.

“Communities relying heavily on rainwater are particularly vulnerable.”

Meanwhile, 'Atuekaho expressed concern for livestock such as cattle, pigs, poultry which are affected by hotter and drier weather. "The possible impacts include, heat stress, reduced growth and productivity, limited pasture availabilities, and higher disease risks."

He mentioned that during the previous El Niño in 2025, some livestock around Tonga died from heat stress.

Last week, the Tonga Meteorological Services ( TMS ) announced a high likelihood for Tropical Cyclone ( TC ) activities affecting Tonga, with increased frequency of severe tropical cyclones category 3 to 5 expected during the upcoming tropical cyclone season, November 2026 - April 2027.

" These cyclones can cause crop destruction, flooding, damage to agricultural infrastructure, and loss of livestock."

On the other hand, marine and fisheries impacts from El Niño may cause coral bleaching, fish migration, and reduced coastal fish availability.

“Farmers, fishermen, and women should be ready and vigilant every year."

" Farmers should plant all year round. Farmers should grow crops to harvest before the cyclone season arrive," 'Atuekaho said.

“Agricultural planning is very important.”

item.title: Falling diesel prices begin to lower the electricity tariff
item.description: 1 July 2026 - Electricity gets noticeably cheaper from today. The Tonga Energy Commission has approved a lower electricity tariff from 1 July 2026, bringing the Regulated Tariff applicable to Tonga Power Limited down from 125.02 to 111.57 seniti per kilowatt-hour (seniti/kWh). That is a fall of 10.8 per cent. The reason for the reduction is that the price of diesel for power generation fell sharpl
item.content:encoded:
1 July 2026 - Electricity gets noticeably cheaper from today. The Tonga Energy Commission has approved a lower electricity tariff from 1 July 2026, bringing the Regulated Tariff applicable to Tonga Power Limited down from 125.02 to 111.57 seniti per kilowatt-hour (seniti/kWh). That is a fall of 10.8 per cent.

The reason for the reduction is that the price of diesel for power generation fell sharply for July, after three months of steep rises. Global fuel prices have started to fall as tensions ni the Middle East war ease and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz cautiously commences. That easing is now reaching Tonga, and ti is what brought the July diesel price down.

Diesel still generates around two-thirds of Tonga's electricity, so when the price of diesel drops, the cost of making power drops with it, and the Commission is focussed on passing that saving straight on to customers.

" This is welcome relief for families and businesses after a hard few months," said Col (Ret'd) Siamelie Latu, Chief Executive Officer of the Commission. "When fuel prices rose, the electricity are rising or falling."

Electricity prices in Tonga rose sharply in May and June, when the conflict in the Middle East disrupted global shipping and pushed fuel prices materially higher. July's reduction, driven by the lower diesel price, reverses part of those increases.

The electricity tariff that Tonga Power Limited charges its customers is regulated by the Commission. Under the regulatory framework, the fuel component is reviewed every quarter so that changes ni the diesel price, up or down, are reflected ni what customers pay. When there is a sudden shock in the cost of fuel between those quarterly reviews, Tonga Power can ask the Commission to approve an out-of-cycle adjustment, and the Commission can require the tariff to come down when fuel prices fal sharply.
